items.headline: Inflation Cools, But Geopolitical Tensions & Oil Prices Keep Markets Mixed
items.summary: U.S. equities saw mixed performance today, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 trending higher while the Dow edged down. Markets reacted positively to a lower-than-expected June CPI report, easing inflation concerns and significantly reducing expectations for a Federal Reserve rate hike this month. However, escalating U.S.-Iran tensions and surging oil prices continued to weigh on overall sentiment, creating a volatile trading environment. Tech stocks experienced a mixed day, with some major names rebounding, while IBM plunged after a disappointing earnings report. Major banks reported strong quarterly profits, bolstering the financial sector. Not financial advice.

items.headline: IBM Plunges on Q2 Miss
items.summary: IBM shares tumbled over 25% after the company reported preliminary second-quarter earnings and revenue below analyst expectations. CEO Arvind Krishna attributed the shortfall to clients shifting spending towards hardware (servers, storage, memory) due to the AI boom, impacting software and infrastructure sales.

items.headline: AppLovin Falls on Growth Concerns
items.summary: AppLovin Corporation saw its shares fall 12.7% after Bank of America reportedly flagged slower June growth in its e-commerce footprint.

items.headline: CrowdStrike Surges
items.summary: CrowdStrike Holdings shares surged 9.4% today. Specific catalysts for today's move were not immediately clear, but cybersecurity remains a key area of focus for many investors.

items.headline: Goldman Sachs Delivers Strong Earnings
items.summary: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. gained 7.4% after reporting record Q2 net revenues of $20.34 billion and net earnings of $6.63 billion, with diluted EPS of $20.98. CEO David Solomon highlighted strong performance across global banking and markets.

items.headline: June CPI Shows Inflation Cooling More Than Expected
items.summary: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) for June 2026 rose 3.5% year-over-year, coming in below economists' expectations of 3.8% (or 3.9%). On a monthly basis, CPI fell 0.4%, marking its largest single-month decline since April 2020. This data has eased inflation concerns and led traders to significantly lower expectations for a Federal Reserve rate hike in July. Core inflation, excluding volatile food and energy, was unchanged from May, with a 12-month rate of 2.6%.

items.headline: Fed Chair Warsh: 'No Tolerance' for High Inflation, Silent on Next Steps
items.summary: Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh testified before Congress, reiterating the Fed's 'no tolerance' for persistently elevated inflation and its commitment to restoring price stability. However, he offered no explicit hints regarding the central bank's immediate policy actions. Warsh acknowledged AI investment as a significant economic feature, with the Fed monitoring its implications for inflation and the labor market.

items.headline: U.S.-Iran Tensions Drive Oil Price Surge
items.summary: Geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and Iran escalated, with President Trump announcing the reinstatement of a blockade on Iranian ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz and proposing a 'protection tax' on cargo. This news sent oil prices surging, with West Texas Intermediate (WTI) climbing to $80.05 a barrel and Brent crude reaching $85.00 to $86.64 a barrel. The ongoing conflict continues to be a significant factor influencing market sentiment.

items.headline: Major Banks Report Strong Q2 Earnings, Bolstering Financial Sector
items.summary: Several major U.S. banks kicked off earnings season with strong second-quarter results. JPMorgan Chase reported diluted EPS of $6.14 on $58.020 billion in revenue, exceeding analyst estimates, with trading revenue soaring amid market volatility. Goldman Sachs posted record net revenues and earnings, with diluted EPS of $20.98. Bank of America and Wells Fargo also released their Q2 results, contributing to an optimistic outlook for the financial sector, though Wells Fargo shares saw a slight decline.

items.headline: Energy Leads, Tech Sees Mixed Fortunes Amidst Sector Rotation
items.summary: The Energy sector emerged as a leader today, with the Energy Select Sector SPDR (XLE) rising 3.2% or 0.64%. Other leading sectors included Industrials (up 0.63%), Materials (up 0.43%), Real Estate (up 0.40%), and Utilities (up 0.24%). Technology experienced mixed fortunes; while some tech stocks rebounded, the Information Technology Select Sector SPDR (XLK) fell 2.1%, and Communication Services (XLC) dropped 1%, indicating a rotation out of parts of the technology sector, particularly among chipmakers. Financials (-0.46%) and Healthcare (-0.44%) were among the lagging sectors.
